key_claims.claim: SVMs were invented by Vladimir Vapnik, with the original optimal hyperplane algorithm dating to 1963 in the Soviet Union. The modern soft-margin version was published by Corinna Cortes and Vapnik in 1995 while at AT&T Bell Labs.

SVMs work by finding the hyperplane that maximizes the margin between classes. The 'kernel trick' allows them to handle non-linear data by implicitly mapping to higher-dimensional spaces where linear separation becomes possible.

Random Forests were introduced by Leo Breiman at UC Berkeley in 2001. He had previously introduced bagging in 1996; Random Forests added random feature selection at each split to reduce correlation between trees.

Random Forests combine multiple decision trees trained on bootstrapped samples, with each tree using a random subset of features. The ensemble averages predictions, reducing overfitting while maintaining accuracy.

Yann LeCun introduced the first practical CNN in 1989 at Bell Labs, applying backpropagation to handwritten zip code recognition. This was the first demonstration that neural networks could learn features directly from raw image pixels.

In the 2010 and 2011 ImageNet competitions, traditional ML methods like SVMs were winning. Starting in 2012, CNNs dominated—AlexNet achieved a 15.3% top-5 error rate versus 26.2% for the second-place entry.

AlexNet was developed by Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever, and Geoffrey Hinton at the University of Toronto. Its success triggered an industry-wide pivot to deep learning, with Google, Facebook, and others racing to adopt neural networks.

The Transformer architecture was introduced by Vaswani et al. at Google in 2017. It replaced recurrence with self-attention mechanisms, allowing each token to attend to every other token in a sequence.
BERT was introduced by Google in October 2018. By December 2019, it had been adopted for over 70 languages on Google Search. By October 2020, almost every English query was processed by a BERT model.

Traditional ML models typically use thousands to millions of parameters. LLMs use billions to trillions of parameters—GPT-4 is estimated at around 1 trillion parameters, roughly 3,000 times larger than BERT's original 340 million.
key_claims.confidence: Medium-High (GPT-4 parameter count is estimated, not officially confirmed)

key_claims.claim: The key architectural difference: traditional ML requires manual feature engineering (humans decide what patterns to look for), while deep learning learns features automatically from raw data. LLMs extend this by learning from raw text at massive scale using self-supervised learning.

XGBoost was introduced by Tianqi Chen and Carlos Guestrin in 2016. It became the dominant algorithm for Kaggle competitions on structured data, frequently outperforming deep neural networks on tabular datasets.

topic: The progression of major AI methods from the 2010s to today - CNNs, Random forest, SVMs, etc. What these each mean, what they're particularly good for, and how they differ from today's LLMs in design and usage
